Gender transition surgery.--The term ``gender transition surgery'' means any medical or surgical service that seeks to surgically alter or remove healthy physical or anatomical characteristics or features that are typical for the individual's biological sex in order to instill or create physiological or anatomical characteristics that resemble a sex different from the individual's birth sex, including genital or non-genital gender reassignment surgery performed for the purpose of assisting an individual with a gender transition.(7) Genital gender transition surgery.--The term ``genital gender transition surgery'' includes surgical procedures such as penectomy, orchiectomy, vaginoplasty, clitoroplasty, or vulvoplasty for biologically male patients or hysterectomy, ovariectomy, reconstruction of the fixed part of the urethra with or without a metoidioplasty or a phalloplasty, vaginectomy, scrotoplasty, or implantation of erection or testicular prostheses for biologically female patients, when performed for the purpose of assisting an individual with a gender transition.(8) Non-genital gender transition surgery.--The term ``non-genital gender transition surgery''--(A) includes, when performed for the purpose of assisting an individual with a gender transition--(i) surgical procedures such as augmentation mammoplasty, facial feminization surgery, liposuction, lipofilling, voice surgery, thyroid cartilage reduction, gluteal augmentation (implants or lipofilling), hair reconstruction, or various aesthetic procedures for biologically male patients; or(ii) subcutaneous mastectomy, voice surgery, liposuction, lipofilling, pectoral implants or various aesthetic procedures for biologically female patients; and(B) does not include any procedure undertaken because the individual suffers from a physical disorder, physical injury, or physical illness that would, as certified by a physician, place the individual in imminent danger of death or impairment of major bodily function unless surgery is performed, unless the procedure is for the purpose of a gender transition.(9) Puberty-blocking drugs.--The term ``puberty-blocking drugs'' means, when used to delay or suppress pubertal development in children for the purpose of assisting an individual with a gender transition--(A) Gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) analogues or other synthetic drugs used in biological males to stop luteinizing hormone secretion and therefore testosterone secretion; and(B) synthetic drugs used in biological females that stop the production of estrogen and progesterone.(10)


Sex; birth sex; biological sex.--The terms ``sex'', ``birth sex,'' and ``biological sex'' refer to the biological indication of male and female in the context of reproductive potential or capacity, such as sex chromosomes, naturally occurring sex hormones, gonads, and non-ambiguous internal and external genitalia present at birth, without regard to an individual's psychological, chosen, or subjective experience of gender.


&lt;all&gt;</pre></body></html>"b"Congressional Bills 118th CongressFrom the U.S. Government Publishing OfficeH.R. 3101 Introduced in House (IH)&lt;DOC&gt;118th CONGRESS1st SessionH. R. 3101To prohibit the issuance of a passport with any gender designationother than ``male'' or ``female'', and for other purposes.


IN THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVESMay 5, 2023 Mrs. Lesko (for herself, Mr. Duncan, Mrs. Miller of Illinois, and Mr. Biggs) introduced the following bill; which was referred to the Committee on Foreign Affairs A BILL To prohibit the issuance of a passport with any gender designationother than ``male'' or ``female'', and for other purposes.


Be it enacted by the Senate and House of Representatives of the United States of America in Congress assembled,SECTION 1.


SHORT TITLE.This Act may be cited as the ``Traditional Passport Act'' or the ``TPA Act''.


SEC.


2. PROHIBITION.(a)


In General.--The Secretary of State may not issue any passport that contains a gender designation other than ``male'' or ``female'', and may permit applicants for passports to select, between such designations, only the designation that matches the biological gender of the applicant.(b) Definition of Biological Gender.--For purposes of the prohibition in this section, the biological gender of an individual means the biological classification of the individual as of the male or female sex in the context of reproductive potential or capacity, as indicated by sex chromosomes, naturally occurring sex hormones, gonads, and nonambiguous internal and external genital

    print(token)

[b"Congressional Bills 118th CongressFrom the U.S. Government Publishing OfficeH.R. 3329 Introduced in House (IH)&lt;DOC&gt;118th CONGRESS1st SessionH. R. 3329To prohibit taxpayer-funded gender transition procedures, and for otherpurposes.,
 IN THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVESMay 15, 2023Mr.,
 LaMalfa (for himself, Mrs. Boebert, Mr. Lamborn, Mr. Gosar, Mr. Banks, Mr. Duncan, Mr. Babin, Mr. Rouzer, Mr. Barr, Mr. Rosendale, Mr. Weber of Texas, Mr. Brecheen, Mr. Norman, Mrs. Miller of Illinois, Mr.Grothman, Mr. Wilson of South Carolina, Mr. Mills, Mr. Burlison, Mr.Smith of New Jersey, Mr. Aderholt, Mrs. McClain, Mrs. Lesko, Mrs.Harshbarger, Mr. Nehls, Mr. Kelly of Mississippi, Mr. Ogles, Mr.Fallon, Mr. LaTurner, Mr. Davidson, Mr. Gaetz, Ms. Tenney, Mr. Allen,Mr. Roy, and Mr. Mooney) introduced the following bill; which wasreferred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committees on the Judiciary, and Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committeeconcerned A BILL To prohibit taxpayer-funded gender transition procedures, and for otherpurposes.,
 Be it enacted by the Senate and House of Representatives of the United States of America in Congress assembled,SECTION 1.,
 SHORT TITLE; TABLE OF CONTENTS.(a)]

# print the lowercase version of the token, 
# and the part of speech (noun, adverb, adjective, etc)
# and the grammatical "head" or subject of the clause/sentence
# and a new line (/n)

for token in doc[:10]:
    print(f'token: {token}')
    print(f'part of speech: {token.pos_}') 
    print(f'grammatical head: {token.head}')
    print('\n')

b"Congressional Bills 118th CongressFrom the U.S. Government Publishing OfficeH.R. 3329 Introduced in House (IH)&lt;DOC&gt;118th CONGRESS1st SessionH. R. 3329To prohibit taxpayer-funded gender transition procedures, and for otherpurposes.

what would I want to look at?#

  • definitions of “gender”, “sex”, “sexuality”

print Named Entities in doc object#

# use doc.ents to access named entities

for ent in doc.ents[:20]:
    print(ent, ent.label_)

remove some ents#

  • People’s names: PERSON

  • numbers: ORDINAL, CARDINAL, DATE, TIME

def remove_ents(text):
    nents = []
    take_out = ['PERSON', 'ORDINAL', 'CARDINAL', 'DATE', 'TIME']
    for item in text.ents:
        if item.label_ not in take_out:
            nents.append(item)
    return nents

nents = remove_ents(doc)

nents[:10]

[the U.S. Government Publishing OfficeH.R. 3329 Introduced,
 House,
 HOUSE,
 Texas,
 Illinois,
 South Carolina,
 New Jersey,
 Mississippi,
 the Committee on Energy and Commerce,
 Ways and Means]

adding entity ruler for GENDER terms#

#Create the EntityRuler
ruler = nlp.add_pipe("entity_ruler", before="ner")

# should I separate out 'gender' from 'sex' from 'orientation'? maybe not
#List of Entities and Patterns
patterns = [
                {"label": "GENDER", "pattern": 'gender'},
                {"label": "GENDER", "pattern": 'trans'},
                {"label": "GENDER", "pattern": 'nonbinary'},
                {"label": "GENDER", "pattern": 'male'},
                {"label": "GENDER", "pattern": 'female'},
                {"label": "SEX", "pattern": 'sex'},
                {"label": "SEX", "pattern": 'biological'},
                {"label": "SEXUALITY", "pattern": 'sexuality'},
                {"label": "SEXUALITY", "pattern": 'orientation'},
                {"label": "SEXUALITY", "pattern": 'queer'},
                {"label": "IDENTITY", "pattern": 'LGBTQ'},
                {"label": "IDENTITY", "pattern": 'LGBT'},
                {"label": "IDENTITY", "pattern": 'LGBTQIA+'},
                {"label": "IDENTITY", "pattern": 'queer'}
            ]

ruler.add_patterns(patterns)

# then load the doc
doc = nlp(text)

#extract entities
for ent in doc.ents[:50]:
    print (ent.text, ent.label_)

b"Congressional Bills 118th CongressFrom the U.S. Government Publishing OfficeH.R. 3329 Introduced ORG in House ORG (IH)&lt;DOC&gt;118th CONGRESS1st SessionH. R. 3329To prohibit taxpayer-funded gender GENDER transition procedures, and for otherpurposes.IN THE HOUSE ORG OF REPRESENTATIVESMay 15, 2023Mr DATE . LaMalfa PERSON (for himself, Mrs. Boebert PERSON , Mr. Lamborn PERSON , Mr. Gosar PERSON , Mr. Banks PERSON , Mr. Duncan PERSON , Mr. Babin PERSON , Mr. Rouzer PERSON , Mr. Barr PERSON , Mr. Rosendale PERSON , Mr. Weber PERSON of Texas GPE , Mr. Brecheen PERSON , Mr. Norman PERSON , Mrs. Miller PERSON of Illinois GPE , Mr. Grothman PERSON , Mr. Wilson PERSON of South Carolina GPE , Mr. Mills PERSON , Mr. Burlison PERSON , Mr. Smith PERSON of New Jersey GPE , Mr. Aderholt PERSON , Mrs. McClain PERSON , Mrs. Lesko PERSON , Mrs. Harshbarger PERSON , Mr. Nehls PERSON , Mr. Kelly PERSON of Mississippi GPE , Mr. Ogles PERSON , Mr. Fallon PERSON , Mr. LaTurner PERSON , Mr. Davidson PERSON , Mr. Gaetz PERSON , Ms. Tenney PERSON , Mr. Allen PERSON ,Mr. Roy PERSON , and Mr. Mooney PERSON ) introduced the following bill; which wasreferred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce ORG , and in addition to the Committees on the Judiciary, and Ways and Means ORG , for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committeeconcerned A BILL To prohibit taxpayer-funded gender GENDER transition procedures, and for otherpurposes.Be it enacted by the Senate ORG and House of Representatives ORG of the United States of America GPE in Congress ORG assembled, SECTION 1 LAW . SHORT TITLE ORG ; TABLE OF CONTENTS.(a) Short Title.--This Act may be cited as the ``End Taxpayer Funding of Gender Experimentation Act PRODUCT of 2023''.(b) Table of Contents.--The table of contents of this Act are as follows: Sec ORG . 1 CARDINAL . Short title; table of contents. TITLE I--PROHIBITING FEDERALLY FUNDED GENDER TRANSITION PROCEDURESSec. 101 CARDINAL . Prohibiting taxpayer-funded gender GENDER transition procedures. Sec ORG . 102 CARDINAL . Amendment to table of chapters.TITLE II--APPLICATION UNDER THE AFFORDABLE CARE ACTSec. 201 CARDINAL . Clarifying application of prohibition to premium credits and cost-sharing reductions under ACA ORG . TITLE I--PROHIBITING FEDERALLY FUNDED GENDER TRANSITION PROCEDURESSEC. 101 CARDINAL . PROHIBITING TAXPAYER-FUNDED GENDER TRANSITION PROCEDURES.Title 1 CARDINAL , United States ORG Code PERSON , is amended by adding at the end the following new chapter: `` CHAPTER 4--PROHIBITING LAW TAXPAYER-FUNDED GENDER TRANSITION PROCEDURES``301. Prohibition on funding for gender GENDER transition procedures.``302. Prohibition on funding for health benefits plans that cover gender GENDER transition procedures.``303. Limitation on Federal facilities and employees.``304. Construction relating to separate coverage.``305. Construction relating to the use of non-Federal funds for health coverage.``306. Construction relating to complications arising from gender GENDER transition procedures. ``307 PERSON . Definitions.``Sec. 301 CARDINAL . Prohibition on funding for gender GENDER transition procedures``No funds authorized or appropriated by Federal law, and none of the funds in any trust fund to which funds are authorized or appropriated by Federal law, shall be expended for any gender GENDER transition procedures.``Sec. 302 CARDINAL . Prohibition on funding for health benefits plans that cover gender GENDER transition procedures``No funds authorized or appropriated by Federal law, and none of the funds in any trust fund to which funds are authorized or appropriated by Federal law, shall be expended for health benefits coverage that includes coverage of gender GENDER transition procedures.``Sec. 303 CARDINAL . Limitation on Federal facilities and employees``No health care service furnished--``(1 PERSON ) by or in a health care facility owned or operated by the Federal Government ORG ; or``(2) by any physician or other individual employed by the Federal Government ORG to provide health care services within the scope of the physician's or individual's employment,may include gender GENDER transition procedures.``Sec. 304 CARDINAL . Construction relating to separate coverage``Nothing in this chapter shall be construed as prohibiting any individual, entity, State ORG , or locality from purchasing separate coverage for gender GENDER transition procedures or health benefits coverage that include gender GENDER transition procedures so long as such coverage is paid for entirely with funds not authorized or appropriated by Federal law, and such coverage shall not be purchased using matching funds required for a federally subsidized program, including a State ORG or locality's contribution of Medicaid ORG matching funds.``Sec. 305 CARDINAL . Construction relating to the use of non-Federal funds for health coverage``Nothing in this chapter shall be construed as restricting the ability of any non-Federal ORG health benefits coverage provider from offering coverage for gender GENDER transition procedures, or the ability of a State ORG or locality to contract separately with such a provider for such coverage, so long as only funds not authorized or appropriated by Federal law are used, and such coverage shall not be purchased using matching funds required for a federally subsidized program, including a State ORG or locality's contribution of Medicaid ORG matching funds.``Sec. 306 CARDINAL . Construction relating to complications arising from gender GENDER transition procedures``Nothing in this chapter shall be construed to apply to the treatment of any infection, injury, disease, or disorder that has been caused by or exacerbated by the performance of a gender GENDER transition procedure.``Sec. 307 CARDINAL . Definitions``For purposes of this chapter:``(1) Biological sex.--The term ` biological SEX sex SEX ' means the biological SEX indication of male GENDER or female GENDER in the context of reproductive potential or capacity, such as sex SEX chromosomes, naturally occurring sex SEX hormones, gonads, and non-ambiguous internal and external genitalia present at birth, without regard to an individual's psychological, chosen, or subjective experience of gender GENDER .``(2) Cross- sex SEX hormones.--The term `cross- sex SEX hormones' means--``(A) testosterone or other androgens given to biological SEX females at doses that are profoundly larger or more potent than would naturally occur in healthy biological SEX females; or``(B) estrogen PERSON given to biological SEX males at doses that are profoundly larger or more potent than would naturally occur in healthy biological SEX males.``(3) Gender.--The term ` gender GENDER ' means the psychological, behavioral, social, and cultural aspects of being male GENDER or female GENDER .``(4) Gender transition.--The term ` gender GENDER transition' means the process in which a person goes from identifying with and living as a gender GENDER that corresponds to his or her biological SEX sex SEX to identifying with and living as a gender GENDER different from his or her biological SEX sex SEX , and may involve social, legal, or physical changes.``(5) Gender transition procedure.--``(A) In general.--The term ` gender GENDER transition procedure' means any medical or surgical service, including physician's services, inpatient and outpatient hospital services, or prescribed drugs related to gender GENDER transition, that seek to alter or remove physical or anatomical characteristics or features that are typical for an individual's biological SEX sex SEX , or to instill or create physiological or anatomical characteristics that resemble a sex SEX different from an individual's birth sex SEX , including, without limitation, medical services that provide puberty-blocking drugs, cross- sex SEX hormones, or other mechanisms to promote the development of feminizing or masculinizing features (in the opposite sex SEX ), or genital gender GENDER transition surgery or nongenital gender GENDER transition surgery performed for the purpose of assisting an individual with a gender GENDER transition.``(B) Exceptions.--The term ` gender GENDER transition procedure' does not include--``(i) services to those born with a medically-verifiable disorder of sex SEX development, including a person with external biological SEX sex SEX characteristics that are irresolvably ambiguous, such as those born with 46 CARDINAL XX chromosomes with virilization, 46 CARDINAL XY ORG chromosomes with undervirilization, or having both ovarian and testicular tissue; or services provided when a physician has otherwise diagnosed a disorder of sexual development, in which such physician has determined through genetic or biochemical testing that the person does not have normal sex SEX chromosome structure, sex SEX steroid hormone production, or sex SEX steroid hormone action for a biological SEX male GENDER or biological SEX female GENDER ; or``(ii) the treatment of any infection, injury, disease, or disorder that has been caused by or exacerbated by the performance of a gender GENDER transition procedure, whether or not such procedure was performed in accordance with State ORG or Federal law, or whether funding for such procedure was permissible under this chapter.``(6) Gender transition surgery.--``(A) In general.--The term ` gender GENDER transition surgery' means any medical or surgical service that seeks to surgically alter or remove healthy physical or anatomical characteristics or features that are typical for an individual's biological SEX sex SEX in order to instill or create physiological or anatomical characteristics that resemble a sex SEX different from an individual's birth sex SEX , including, without limitation, genital gender GENDER reassignment surgery or non-genital gender GENDER reassignment surgery performed for the purpose of assisting an individual with a gender GENDER transition.``(B) Exception.--Gender transition surgery does not include any procedure undertaken because the individual suffers from a physical disorder, physical injury, or physical illness that would, as certified by a physician, place the individual in imminent danger of death or impairment of major bodily function unless surgery is performed.``(7) Genital gender GENDER transition surgery.--The term `genital gender GENDER transition surgery' includes surgical procedures such as penectomy, orchiectomy, vaginoplasty, clitoroplasty, or vulvoplasty for biologically male GENDER patients, or hysterectomy/ovariectomy, reconstruction of the fixed part of the urethra with or without a metoidioplasty or a phalloplasty, vaginectomy, scrotoplasty, or implantation of erection or testicular prostheses for biologically female GENDER patients when performed for the purpose of assisting an individual with a gender GENDER transition.``(8) Non-genital gender GENDER transition surgery.--The term `non-genital gender GENDER transition surgery' means surgical procedures including augmentation mammoplasty, facial feminization surgery, liposuction, lipofilling, voice surgery, thyroid cartilage reduction, gluteal augmentation (implants or lipofilling), hair reconstruction, or other aesthetic procedures for biologically male GENDER patients or subcutaneous mastectomy, voice surgery, liposuction, lipofilling, pectoral implants, or other aesthetic procedures for biologically female GENDER patients, when performed for the purpose of assisting an individual with a gender GENDER transition.``(9) Puberty-blocking drugs.--The term `puberty-blocking drugs' means--``(A) Gonadotropin PERSON -releasing hormone (GnRH) analogues or other synthetic drugs used in biological SEX males to stop luteinizing hormone secretion and therefore testosterone secretion;``(B) synthetic drugs used in biological SEX females that stop the production of estrogen CARDINAL ; and``(C) progesterone when used to delay or suppress pubertal development in children for the purpose of assisting an individual with a gender GENDER transition.''. SEC ORG . 102 CARDINAL . AMENDMENT PERSON TO TABLE OF CHAPTERS.The table of chapters for title 1 CARDINAL , United States ORG Code PERSON , is amended by adding at the end the following new item:``4.Prohibiting taxpayer-funded gender GENDER transition301''.procedures.TITLE II--APPLICATION UNDER THE AFFORDABLE CARE ACTSEC. 201 CARDINAL . CLARIFYING APPLICATION OF PROHIBITION TO PREMIUM CREDITS AND COST-SHARING REDUCTIONS UNDER ACA.(a PRODUCT ) In General.--(1) Disallowance of refundable credit and cost-sharing reductions for coverage under qualified health plan which provides coverage for gender GENDER transition procedures.--(A) In general.--Section 36B(c)(3)(A ORG ) of the Internal Revenue Code ORG of 1986 DATE is amended by inserting before the period at the end the following: ``or any health plan that includes coverage for gender GENDER transition procedures (other than any procedure described in section 306 or LAW 307 CARDINAL of title 1 CARDINAL , United States GPE Code)''.(B) Option to purchase or offer separate coverage or plan.--Section 36B(c)(3 CARDINAL ) of such Code PERSON is amended by adding at the end the following new subparagraph:``(C) Separate coverage or plan for gender GENDER transition procedures allowed.--``(i) Option to purchase separate coverage or plan.--Nothing in subparagraph (A) shall be construed as prohibiting any individual from purchasing separate coverage for gender GENDER transition procedures described in such subparagraph, or a health plan that includes such gender GENDER transition procedures, so long as no credit is allowed under this section with respect to the premiums for such coverage or plan.``(ii) Option to offer coverage or plan.--Nothing in subparagraph (A) shall restrict any non-Federal ORG health insurance issuer offering a health plan from offering separate coverage for gender GENDER transition procedures described in such subparagraph, or a plan that includes such gender GENDER transition procedures, so long as premiums for such separate coverage or plan are not paid for with any amount attributable to the credit allowed under this section (or the amount of any advance payment of the credit under section 1412 LAW of the Patient Protection ORG and Affordable Care Act).''.(2) Disallowance of small employer health insurance expense credit for plan which includes coverage for gender GENDER transition procedures.--Subsection (h) of section 45R CARDINAL of the Internal Revenue Code ORG of 1986 DATE is amended--(A ORG ) by striking ``Any term'' and inserting the following:``(1) In general.--Any term''; and(B ORG ) by adding at the end the following new paragraph:``(2) Exclusion of health plans including coverage for gender GENDER transition procedures.--``(A) In general.--The term `qualified health plan' does not include any health plan that includes coverage for gender GENDER transition procedures (other than a procedure described in section 306 or LAW 307 CARDINAL of title 1 CARDINAL , United States GPE Code).``(B) Separate coverage or plan for gender GENDER transition procedures allowed.--``(i) Option to purchase separate coverage or plan.--Nothing in subparagraph (A) shall be construed as prohibiting any employer from purchasing for its employees separate coverage for gender GENDER transition procedures described in such subparagraph, or a health plan that includes such gender GENDER transition procedures, so long as no credit is allowed under this section with respect to the employer contributions for such coverage or plan.``(ii) Option to offer coverage or plan.--Nothing in subparagraph (A) shall restrict any non-Federal ORG health insurance issuer offering a health plan from offering separate coverage for gender GENDER transition procedures described in such subparagraph, or a plan that includes such gender GENDER transition procedures, so long as such separate coverage or plan is not paid for with any employer contribution eligible for the credit allowed under this section.''.(b) Application to Multi-State Plans.--Section 1334(a GPE ) of the Patient Protection ORG and Affordable Care Act ( 42 CARDINAL U.S.C. 18054(a GPE )) is amended by adding at the end the following new paragraph:``(8) Coverage consistent with federal policy regarding gender GENDER transition procedures.--In entering into contracts under this subsection, the Director shall ensure that no multi-State qualified health plan offered in an Exchange ORG provides health benefits coverage for which the expenditure of Federal funds is prohibited under chapter 4 LAW of title 1, United States ORG Code.''.(c) Effective Date.--(1) Effective date with respect to premium credits and cost sharing reductions.--The amendments made by subsection (a) shall apply to taxable years DATE after the date that is one year DATE after the date of enactment of this Act, but only with respect to plan years DATE beginning after such date.(2) Effective date for application to multi-state plans.--The amendment made by subsection (b) shall apply to plan years DATE beginning on the date that is one year DATE after the date of enactment of this Act. &lt;all&gt;</pre></body></html>"b"Congressional Bills 118th CongressFrom PERSON the U.S. Government Publishing ORG OfficeH.R. 345 CARDINAL Introduced in House ORG (IH)&lt;DOC&gt;118th CONGRESS1st SessionH. R. 345To require Members of Congress ORG and their spouses and dependent childrento place certain assets into blind trusts, and for other purposes.IN THE HOUSE ORG OF REPRESENTATIVESJanuary 12, 2023 Ms. Spanberger PERSON (for herself, Mr. Roy PERSON , Mr. Cleaver PERSON , Mr. Cloud PERSON , Mr. Courtney PERSON , Ms. Craig PERSON , Mr. Crow PERSON , Ms. Dean PERSON of Pennsylvania GPE , Mr. Fitzpatrick PERSON , Mr. Gaetz PERSON , Mr. Golden PERSON of Maine GPE , Mr. Gottheimer PERSON , Mr. Grijalva PERSON , Ms. Houlahan PERSON , Mr. Johnson PERSON of South Dakota GPE , Mr. Kildee PERSON , Mr. Kilmer PERSON , Mrs. Kim PERSON of California GPE , Ms. Leger Fernandez PERSON , Ms. McCollum PERSON , Ms. Meng PERSON , Mr. Nadler PERSON , Mr. Pappas PERSON , Ms. Perez PERSON , Mr. Perry PERSON , Mr. Phillips PERSON , Ms. Pingree PERSON , Ms. Porter PERSON , Ms. Scanlon PERSON , Mr. Schiff PERSON , Mr. Sessions PERSON , Ms. Slotkin PERSON , Ms. Stevens PERSON , Ms. Titus PERSON , Mr. Tonko PERSON , Ms. Wild PERSON , Ms. Williams PERSON ofGeorgia, and Mr. Magaziner PERSON ) introduced the following bill; which wasreferred to the Committee on ORG House Administration A BILL To require Members of Congress ORG and their spouses and dependent childrento place certain assets into blind trusts, and for other purposes.Be it enacted by the Senate ORG and House of Representatives ORG of the United States of America GPE in Congress ORG assembled, SECTION 1 LAW . SHORT TITLE.This Act may be cited as the ``Transparent Representation Upholding Service and Trust PRODUCT in Congress Act'' or the ``TRUST PRODUCT in Congress ORG Act'' PRODUCT . SEC ORG . 2 CARDINAL . PLACEMENT OF CERTAIN ASSETS OF MEMBERS OF CONGRESS ORG AND THEIR SPOUSES AND DEPENDENT CHILDREN IN BLIND TRUST.(a ORG ) In General.--(1) Members occupying office on date of enactment.--Not later than 90 days DATE after the date of enactment of this Act, a Member of Congress ORG and any spouse or dependent child of such Member shall place any covered investment owned by such Member, spouse, or dependent child into a qualified blind trust.(2) Members assuming office after date of enactment.--Not later than 90 days DATE after the date an individual becomes a Member of Congress ORG after the date of enactment of this Act, such individual and any spouse or dependent child of such individual shall place any covered investment owned by such individual, spouse, or dependent child into a qualified blind trust.(3) Mingling of assets.--A spouse or dependent child of a Member of Congress ORG may place a covered investment in a qualified blind trust established by such Member under paragraph ( 1 CARDINAL ) or (2).(4) Assets upon separation.--A Member of Congress ORG and any spouse or dependent child of such Member may not dissolve any qualified blind trust in which a covered investment has been placed pursuant to paragraph ( 1 CARDINAL ) or ( 2 CARDINAL ), or otherwise control such an investment, until the date that is 180 days DATE after the date such Member ceases to be a Member of Congress.(b) Accountability.--(1) Members of the house.--A Member of Congress ORG who is a Member of the House of Representatives shall--(A ORG ) not later than 15 days DATE after the date a qualified blind trust is established under subsection (a), certify to the Clerk of the House of Representatives ORG that such trust has been established and that any covered investment owned by such Member or a spouse or dependent child of such Member has been placed in such trust; or(B) certify to the Clerk PERSON that such Member or a spouse or dependent child of such Member does not own any covered investment.(2) Members of the senate.--A Member of Congress ORG who is a Senator shall--(A) not later than 15 days DATE after the date a qualified blind trust is established under subsection (a), certify to the Secretary of the Senate ORG that such trust has been established and that any covered investment owned by such Member or a spouse or dependent child of such Member has been placed in such trust; or(B) certify to the Secretary that such Member or a spouse or dependent child of such Member does not own any covered investment.(3) Report.--The Clerk PERSON of the House of Representatives ORG and the Secretary of the Senate ORG shall make available on the public website of the Clerk PERSON and the Secretary, respectively, any certification made under paragraph ( 1 CARDINAL ) or ( 2).(c CARDINAL ) Exception.--A spouse or dependent child who receives compensation from their primary occupation through any covered investment shall not be required to place such covered investment in a qualified blind trust under this Act.(d) Definitions.--In this Act:(1) Commodity.--The term ``commodity'' has the meaning given the term in section 1a LAW of the Commodity Exchange Act ORG ( 7 U.S.C. 1a).(2 DATE ) Covered investment.--The term ``covered investment''--(A) means investment in a security, a commodity, or a future, or any comparable economic interest acquired through synthetic means such as the use of a derivative; and(B ORG ) does not include--(i) a widely held investment fund described in section 13104(f)(8 LAW ) of title 5 CARDINAL , United States ORG Code; or(ii) a United States ORG Treasury ORG bill, note, or bond.(3) Dependent child.--The term ``dependent child'' has the meaning given that term in section 13101 LAW of title 5, United States GPE Code.(4) Member of congress.--The term ``Member of Congress ORG '' has the meaning given that term in such section 13101.(5 LAW ) Qualified blind trust.--The term ``qualified blind trust'' has the meaning given that term in section 13104(f)(3 LAW ) of such title. &lt;all&gt;</pre></body></html>"b"Congressional Bills 118th CongressFrom PERSON the U.S. Government Publishing OfficeH.R. 4342 Introduced ORG in House ORG (IH)&lt;DOC&gt;118th CONGRESS1st SessionH. R. 4342To support educational entities in fully implementing title IX andreducing and preventing sex SEX discrimination in all areas of education, and for other purposes.IN THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES ORG June 23, 2023Ms DATE . Matsui PERSON (for herself, Ms. DelBene PERSON , Mr. Morelle PERSON , Ms. Norton PERSON , Mr. Kilmer PERSON , Mr. Case PERSON , and Mr. Carbajal PERSON ) introduced the following bill;which PERSON was referred to the Committee on Education ORG and the Workforce ORG A BILL To support educational entities in fully implementing title IX andreducing and preventing sex SEX discrimination in all areas of education, and for other purposes.Be it enacted by the Senate ORG and House of Representatives ORG of the United States of America GPE in Congress ORG assembled, SECTION 1 LAW . SHORT TITLE.This Act may be cited as the `` Patsy T. Mink PERSON and Louise M. Slaughter PERSON Gender Equity in Education Act of 2023'' CARDINAL . SEC ORG . 2. CARDINAL FINDINGS.Congress finds the following:(1) Title IX of the Education Amendments WORK_OF_ART of 1972 DATE ( 20 CARDINAL U.S.C. 1681 DATE et seq.) (in this section referred to as ``title IX'') and the implementing regulations of title IX prohibit sex SEX discrimination in federally funded education programs and activities.(2) Although title IX requires that schools treat students equally with regard to athletic participation opportunities, athletic scholarships, and the benefits and services provided to athletic teams, female GENDER participation rates, especially for girls of color, lag far behind male GENDER participation rates. Nationally, for example, boys receive more than 1,000,000 CARDINAL more opportunities to play high school sports than girls. Furthermore, although girls comprise nearly 50 percent PERCENT of high school students, schools provide them with only 43 percent PERCENT of athletic opportunities, even though girls want to play in greater numbers.(3) A recent report from the Women's Sports Foundation ORG found that Black, Indigenous GPE , and People of Color ORG ( BIPOC ORG ) women still lag behind White women in collegiate sports--14 percent compared to 30 percent PERCENT , respectively. These disparities are also prevalent at the high school level, with fewer athletic opportunities available to students in heavily minority schools compared to heavily White ORG schools. In a typical heavily minority school, there are only 25 CARDINAL athletic spots available for every 100 CARDINAL students, compared to 58 CARDINAL athletic spots for every 100 CARDINAL students in a typical heavily White school. Further broken down by gender GENDER , girls have 82 percent PERCENT of the athletic opportunities that boys do in a heavily White school, compared to 67 percent PERCENT for girls in a heavily minority school.(4) Female student athletes have been found to have higher levels of self-esteem, as well as reduced risk for cardiovascular disease, diabetes, osteoporosis, and breast cancer, compared to female GENDER students who do not play sports. In addition, female GENDER student athletes are more likely to graduate from high school, score higher on standardized tests, and have higher grades than female GENDER students who do not play sports. Girls who play sports in high school go on to earn 7 percent PERCENT higher annual wages than those who do not play sports, and are more likely to enter the labor force and pursue higher-skill, previously male GENDER -dominated positions. Generally, sports participation for women is associated with a lower prevalence of experiencing intimate partner violence, reinforcing that athletic access not only strengthens health, educational, and workplace outcomes, but also personal safety. According to a 2023 DATE report from the Women's Sports Foundation ORG , girls who played sports during the first year DATE of the COVID-19 ORG pandemic fared significantly better than girls who did not, reporting higher levels of self-esteem, self-efficacy, and social support, and lower levels of loneliness and depression.(5) Although the availability of athletic scholarships facilitates access to higher education, many institutions of higher education fail to award proportional athletic financial aid to women, which can affect their long-term employment outcomes and economic security. According to the Department of Education's Equity in Athletics Disclosure Act ORG data from 2019 DATE through 2020 DATE , male GENDER athletes received $ 252,000,000 MONEY more in athletic scholarships than female GENDER athletes.(6) Although title IX ensures gender GENDER equity in career and technical education, women are severely underrepresented in fields nontraditional to their gender GENDER . According to the National Coalition for Women and Girls in Education ORG , women make up more than 80 percent PERCENT of workers with training or certification in historically women-dominated occupations that pay less than $30,000 MONEY per year, including child care, early childhood education, home care, and cosmetology. Women represent less than 40 percent PERCENT of workers trained or certified in high-paying and historically male GENDER -dominated fields, including transportation, advanced manufacturing, and construction.(7) Although title IX ensures gender GENDER equity in the fields of science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (in this section referred to as ``STEM'') education, women are disproportionately lost at nearly every stage of the STEM pipeline. A recent report by the National Center for Education Statistics ORG showed that women earned only 32 percent PERCENT of all STEM degrees in 2017 DATE , and nearly \\1/2\\ of these women were White. Women of color earned about 12 percent PERCENT of STEM degrees in that same year DATE . Furthermore, in STEM fields where women are particularly underrepresented, such as computing and engineering, women earned an even smaller percentage of degrees, including only 19 percent PERCENT of computing bachelor's degrees, and 21 percent PERCENT of engineering bachelor's degrees.(8) Although title IX prohibits sex SEX discrimination in employment in federally funded education programs, according to the National Science Foundation ORG , women only hold 34 percent PERCENT of all tenured and tenure-track positions and 27 percent PERCENT of full professor positions in STEM fields. Furthermore, Black and Latina PERSON women, together, hold only 4 percent PERCENT of all tenured and tenure-track positions and barely over 2 percent PERCENT of full professor positions in STEM fields. Asian-American NORP women hold around 5 percent PERCENT of all tenured and tenure-track positions, and less than 3 percent PERCENT of full professor positions in STEM fields.(9) Although title IX protects against sexual and sex SEX -based harassment and violence, 56 percent PERCENT of girls and 40 percent PERCENT of boys in grades 7 CARDINAL through 12 CARDINAL experience sexual harassment each year DATE , and 9 percent PERCENT of girls and 7 percent PERCENT of boys in high school experience physical dating violence each year DATE . In addition, more than 60 percent PERCENT of women and men in college experience sexual harassment each year DATE , and 14 percent PERCENT of women and 10 percent PERCENT of men in college experience dating violence. Moreover, these statistics are often higher for marginalized students, including Black and Brown WORK_OF_ART girls and women, lesbian NORP , gay, bisexual, transgender, queer SEXUALITY , and questioning (referred to in this section as `` LGBTQ IDENTITY '') students, pregnant and parenting students, and disabled students.(10) According to GLSEN ORG , 87 percent PERCENT of LGBTQ IDENTITY students have experienced harassment or assault based on a personal characteristic, and nearly 66 percent PERCENT have experienced LGBTQ IDENTITY -related verbal harassment at school based on sexual orientation SEXUALITY . Research has shown that LGBTQ IDENTITY students who experience harassment at school are more likely to experience depression and anxiety, to engage in unhealthy and antisocial behaviors, and to have more unexcused absences from school.(11) Although title IX prohibits discrimination on the basis of pregnancy or parenting status, the limited availability of accommodations, including lactation accommodations, excused absences for pregnancy-related medical conditions, and child care needs (including caring for a sick child), is a leading reason that parenting mothers drop out of high school. According to the National Women's Law Center ORG , only half CARDINAL of teenage mothers earn a high school diploma by the age of 22 DATE , compared to 89 percent PERCENT of women who do not have a child during their teenage years DATE , and one-third CARDINAL of young mothers will never get a diploma or a GED ORG , further limiting continuing opportunities for education and employment.(12) Although title IX protects against discrimination based on stereotypes of actual or perceived sex SEX , many people carry implicit or unconscious biases that can unintentionally influence attitudes, beliefs, behaviors, and decision-making processes. Research has shown that unconscious biases can impact classroom environments, teaching methods, student evaluations, disciplinary practices, and career and counseling guidance, which can lead to discrimination against students based on race, color, national origin, and disability, particularly for students who are pursuing nontraditional fields.(13) Nationally, the Feminist Majority Foundation ORG estimates 100,000 CARDINAL title IX coordinators are needed to meet the needs of schools serving children in prekindergarten through grade 12 CARDINAL , local educational agencies, and postsecondary institutions. However, in 2016 DATE , the Department of Education ORG only identified 23,000 CARDINAL title IX coordinators nationwide. The Feminist Majority Foundation ORG has found that schools serving children in prekindergarten through grade 12 CARDINAL rarely have their own title IX coordinators. SEC ORG . 3 CARDINAL . DEFINITIONS.In this Act:(1) ESEA definitions.--The terms ``elementary school'', ``institution of higher education'', ``local educational agency'', ``secondary school'', and `` State ORG educational agency'' have the meanings given those terms in section 8101 LAW of the Elementary PERSON and Secondary Education Act ORG of 1965 DATE ( 20 CARDINAL U.S.C. 7801).(2) Director.--The term ``Director'' means the Director of the Office for Gender Equity ORG established under section 5(a).(3 LAW ) Educational entity.--The term ``educational entity'' means any of the following entities that receive Federal funds:(A) A State ORG educational agency.(B) A local educational agency.(C) An institution of higher education.(D) An elementary school or secondary school.(E) Another entity covered by title IX, such as a laboratory, library, or museum that provides education programs and activities.(4) Gender identity.--The term `` gender GENDER identity'' means the gender GENDER -related identity, appearance, mannerisms, or other gender GENDER -related characteristics of an individual, regardless of the individual's designated sex SEX at birth.(5) National gender GENDER equity infrastructure.--The term ``national gender GENDER equity infrastructure'' means the horizontal and vertical network of title IX coordinators and title IX allies who work to advance gender GENDER equity and eliminate discrimination in the United States.(6) Secretary.--The term ``Secretary'' means the Secretary of Education.(7 GPE ) Sex.--The term `` sex SEX '' includes--(A ORG ) a sex SEX stereotype;(B) pregnancy, childbirth, or a related medical condition;(C) sexual orientation SEXUALITY or gender GENDER identity; and(D) sex SEX characteristics, including intersex traits.(8) Sexual orientation.--The term ``sexual orientation SEXUALITY '' means homosexuality, heterosexuality, or bisexuality.(9) Title ix.--The term ``title IX'' means title IX of the Education Amendments ORG of 1972 DATE ( 20 CARDINAL U.S.C. 1681 et seq.).(10) Title ix ally.--The term ``title IX ally'' means an individual who--(A) is an employee at an educational entity (other than a title IX coordinator), a community stakeholder, or an equity expert; and(B ORG ) helps to fully implement title IX.(11) Title ix coordinator.--The term ``title IX coordinator'' means a responsible employee, as described in section 106.8(a LAW ) of title 34, Code of Federal Regulations, or successor regulations, designated to coordinate efforts under title IX of the Education Amendments ORG of 1972 DATE ( 20 CARDINAL U.S.C. 1681 CARDINAL et seq.).SEC. 4. CARDINAL PURPOSES.The purposes of this Act are to--(1) advance gender GENDER equity in education in the United States;(2 GPE ) support educational entities so that such entities have the support to fully implement title IX;(3) provide title IX coordinators and title IX allies with training, technical assistance, and support to fully carry out their roles and responsibilities;(4) increase general awareness about the rights and obligations of individuals and entities under title IX;(5) identify, implement, and disseminate best practices for reducing and preventing sex SEX discrimination in all areas of education;(6) promote educational environments that are safe and free of sexual and sex SEX -based bullying, harassment, and violence;(7 ORG ) promote equity in education for students and staff who face discrimination based on multiple and intersectional characteristics, including--(A ORG ) race and color;(B) ethnicity;(C) national origin;(D) disability status;(E) religion;(F) age; or(G) actual or perceived sex SEX ; and(8) promote activities that strengthen the national gender GENDER equity infrastructure. SEC ORG . 5. CARDINAL ESTABLISHMENT OF AN OFFICE FOR GENDER EQUITY.(a) In General.--The Secretary shall establish an Office for Gender Equity ORG . The Director of the Office for Gender Equity ORG shall be the Special Assistant for Gender Equity, as authorized under section 202(b)(3 LAW ) of the Department of Education Organization Act ORG ( 20 CARDINAL U.S.C. 3412(b)(3 CARDINAL )). The Director of the Office for Gender Equity ORG shall report directly to the Secretary.(b) Duties.--The Office for Gender Equity shall be responsible for the following:(1) Supporting educational entities in the full implementation of title IX.(2) Providing title IX coordinators and title IX allies with training, technical assistance, and support to fully carry out their roles and responsibilities.(3) Providing grants to implement programs and activities that are focused on reducing and preventing sex SEX discrimination in all areas of education.(4) Identifying and disseminating information and evidence-based best practices for reducing and preventing sex SEX discrimination in all areas of education.(5) Maintaining an Office for Gender Equity resource center website to disseminate information and evidence-based best practices for achieving gender GENDER equity.(6) Performing any other activity consistent with achieving the purposes of this Act.(c) Coordination.--To carry out the purposes of this Act, the Secretary shall coordinate with other relevant Federal offices and agencies, including--(1) the White House ORG Gender Policy Council;(2) the White House ORG Domestic Policy Council;(3) the Office for Civil Rights of the Department of Education;(4 ORG ) the Institute of Education Sciences;(5 ORG ) the Women's Bureau of the Department of Labor;(6 ORG ) the Office on Women's Health of the Department of Health and Human Services;(7 ORG ) the Civil Rights Division ORG of the Department of Justice;(8 ORG ) the Office on Violence Against Women of the Department of Justice;(9 ORG ) the Centers for Disease Control ORG and Prevention;(10) the Office of Safe and Healthy Students ORG of the Department of Education ORG ; and(11) other entities determined relevant for carrying out the purposes of this Act. SEC ORG . 6 CARDINAL . SUPPORT FOR TITLE IX COORDINATORS AND TITLE IX ALLIES.(a) In General.--The Director shall provide coordination, training, technical assistance, and support for title IX coordinators and title IX allies to ensure that educational entities are able to fully implement title IX and reduce and prevent sex SEX discrimination in all areas of education.(b) Title IX Coordinator Training.--(1) In general.--(A) Title ix coordinator training.--Not less than once a year, the Director shall conduct a training for all title IX coordinators, which shall address the different needs of elementary schools and secondary ORDINAL schools and postsecondary institutions. The training may be conducted in partnership with a national organization with relevant expertise, and may be completed online or in person.(B) Availability to title ix allies.--Each training conducted under subparagraph (A) shall be made available to title IX allies to the maximum extent practicable.(2) Contents of training.--The training described in paragraph ( 1 CARDINAL ) shall include the following information:(A) The role and responsibility of title IX coordinators.(B) Information and evidence-based best practices for increasing awareness about rights and obligations under title IX.(C) Information and evidence-based best practices for investigating and responding to claims of violations of title IX.(D) Information and evidence-based best practices for identifying and preventing implicit and explicit sex SEX discrimination in all areas of education, including--(i) recruitment and admissions;(ii) teaching practices, textbooks, and curricula;(iii) campus safety and security;(iv) financial assistance;(v) access to facilities, resources, and housing;(vi ORG ) access to course offerings;(vii) student health services and insurance benefits;(viii) counseling and career guidance;(ix) athletics;(x) discipline policies;(xi) employment; and(xii) other areas that the Director determines are relevant for such purposes.(3) Application of training.--(A) In general.--The Director shall take steps to ensure that the trainings described in paragraph ( 1)--(i CARDINAL ) are adapted, as necessary, to address issues of sex SEX discrimination at all levels of education;(ii) are updated with the latest information and evidence-based best practices; and(iii) address recent trends in sex SEX discrimination.(B) Attention to discrimination based on multiple characteristics.--The Director shall take steps to ensure that such trainings include attention to students who face discrimination based on multiple characteristics, including--(i) race and color;(ii) ethnicity;(iii) national origin;(iv) disability status;(v) religion;(vi PERSON ) age; or(vii) actual or perceived sex.(C) Evaluation.--The Director shall--(i) develop and conduct pre- and post-training evaluations to assess the effectiveness of such trainings in improving the knowledge of the roles and responsibilities of title IX coordinators; and(ii) use such evaluations to update the title IX coordinator trainings annually DATE and replicate effective models and practices for use by title IX coordinators and title IX allies.(c) Handbook for Conducting Title IX Compliance Self-Evaluations.--The Director shall develop a handbook for conducting self-evaluations of compliance with title IX in all areas of education, as described in subsection (b)(2)(D).(d) Assessment of Support for Title IX Coordinators and Title IX Allies.--The Director shall collect relevant data and statistics on all title IX coordinators, including demographic information for gender GENDER , race, and ethnicity, salary information, budgets, and primary roles, in order to make recommendations for improving title IX coordinator support. The assessment shall also describe how title IX coordinators work with title IX allies and others within the national gender GENDER equity infrastructure.(e) Dissemination.--The PERSON Director shall ensure that the workplace contact information of all title IX coordinators and any training materials or information developed under this section are made available on the Office for Gender Equity resource center ORG website described in section 5(b)(5).SEC. LAW 7 CARDINAL . SUPPORT FOR LOCAL IMPLEMENTATION.(a) Grants Authorized.--(1) In general.--The Secretary, acting through the Director, is authorized to award grants to eligible entities to support such eligible entities in fully implementing title IX and reducing and preventing sex SEX discrimination in all areas of education.(2) Eligible entity.--In this section, the term ``eligible entity'' means--(A) a State ORG educational agency;(B) a local educational agency;(C) an institution of higher education;(D) an elementary school or secondary school; or(E) a partnership consisting of--(i) an entity described in subparagraphs (A) through (D); and(ii) a national organization with relevant expertise, or another entity that the Secretary determines has relevant expertise.(b) Use of Funds.--An NORP eligible entity receiving a grant under this section shall use such funds to carry out programs and activities designed to fully implement title IX and prevent and reduce sex SEX discrimination, including programs and activities that--(1) increase awareness of and counteract sex SEX stereotypes, biases, and discrimination;(2) include trainings for students, teachers, faculty, other personnel, and community stakeholders, including title IX allies, to learn about and use best practices for reducing and preventing sex SEX discrimination in all areas of education;(3) increase access to school, campus, and community resources, facilities, and course offerings;(4) support title IX coordinators and title IX allies in performing outreach, advocacy, and education about title IX and reducing and preventing sex SEX discrimination;(5) are aimed at identifying patterns or systemic problems in compliance with title IX;(6) strengthen prevention education and awareness programs regarding sexual and sex SEX -based harassment and violence;(7 ORG ) develop, conduct and analyze evidence-based campus climate and victimization surveys;(8) include institutional assessment activities to identify areas and causes of gender GENDER inequities;(9 ORG ) make efforts to improve progress on gender GENDER equity indicators as described in subsection (c)(2)(A);(10) make efforts to improve accuracy in measurement, data collection, and reporting of gender GENDER equity indicators as described in subsection (c)(2)(A); and(11) make efforts to strengthen the national gender GENDER equity infrastructure (which may include institutions of higher education, State ORG educational agencies, local educational agencies, and individual schools), such as by hiring one or more dedicated employees to serve as title IX coordinators.(c) Applications.--(1) In general.--An eligible entity desiring a grant under this section shall submit an application to the Secretary at such time, in such manner, and containing such information as the Secretary may reasonably require.(2) Contents of application.--Each application submitted by an eligible entity under this section shall include the following:(A) A description of locally defined and documented gender GENDER equity needs and priorities, which may include any of the following indicators:(i) Academic indicators, including performance on State ORG assessments, and enrollment, admission, attrition, time to completion, and graduation rates.(ii) Civil rights data, including statistics on bullying, harassment, violence, discipline, and expulsion.(iii) Campus climate and victimization data.(iv) Employment data.(v) Athletics equity data.(vi) Attendance and absenteeism data.(vii) Evidence of burden on title IX coordinators, including coordinator to student ratio and competing responsibilities.(viii PERSON ) Other documentation of need that the Secretary determines is relevant.(B GPE ) A description of the evidence that will serve as the basis for the activities that the eligible entity proposes to carry out using grant funds under this section.(C) A description of the activities that the eligible entity proposes to carry out using grant funds under this section.(D DATE ) A description of how the proposed activities will be adapted, as necessary, to meet the needs of students who face discrimination based on multiple characteristics, including--(i) race and color;(ii) ethnicity;(iii) national origin;(iv) disability status;(v) religion;(vi PERSON ) age; or(vii) actual or perceived sex.(E) A description of how the proposed activities will help the eligible entity fully implement title IX.(F) A description of a plan for how the proposed activities under this section will continue with local support following completion of the grant period and termination of Federal funding.(G) A description of how the proposed activities are a significant component of a comprehensive plan for gender GENDER equity in education and full implementation of title IX.(d) Rule of Construction.--Nothing in this section shall be construed as prohibiting persons of any sex SEX or gender GENDER from participating in any of the programs or activities funded under this section.(e) Award Basis.--(1) Merit review.--Grants shall be awarded under this section on a competitive basis.(2) Priorities.--(A PERSON ) In general.--The Secretary shall establish criteria for determining which eligible entities shall have priority in receiving a grant under this section.(B) Level of priority.--The criteria described in subparagraph (A) may include a consideration of the extent to which the application demonstrates that the eligible entity--(i) has demonstrated a high need for gender GENDER equity assistance based on indicators described in subsection (c)(2)(A) and a high commitment to addressing these issues;(ii) will address the needs of students who face discrimination based on multiple characteristics, including--(I GPE ) race and color;(II) ethnicity;(III) national origin;(IV GPE ) disability status;(V) religion;(VI PERSON ) age; or(VII) actual or perceived sex;(iii) will address relevant issues of national significance through solutions that can be replicated;(iv) will implement an institutional change strategy with a long-term impact that will continue to be a central activity of the eligible entity upon termination of the grant;(v) will serve a high percentage of low-income students;(vi) will serve a high percentage of racially diverse students;(vii) will promote outreach to include others in their gender GENDER equity training and related activities during the grant period and after the grant ends; and(viii) will impact as many educational entities as possible to advance title IX implementation during and after the grant period.(C) Special ORG rule.--To the extent practicable, the Secretary shall ensure that grants awarded under this section, for each fiscal year DATE , address--(i) all levels of education, including--(I GPE ) elementary and secondary education;(II) undergraduate and graduate education;(III) postdoctoral education and research;(IV) career and technical education; and(V NORP ) adult education;(ii) all regions of the United States GPE ; and(iii) urban, rural, and suburban educational entities.(f) Evaluation and Dissemination.--(1) Evaluation.--(A) In general.--Each eligible entity that receives a grant under this section shall conduct an assessment about the extent to which the eligible entity made progress on the indicators under subsection (c)(2)(A).(B) Assessment.--An eligible entity may work in partnership with the Institute of Education Sciences ORG to conduct such assessment.(C) Use by secretary.--Not later than 1 year DATE after receiving the grant award, the eligible entity shall submit a report to the Secretary containing the results of such assessment. The Secretary shall use those reports in order to build the knowledge base on promising models for preventing and reducing sex SEX discrimination across all areas and levels of education.(2) Dissemination.--The PERSON Secretary shall coordinate with the Director of the Institute of Education Sciences ORG and other relevant Federal offices and agencies to--(A) ensure that the results of the activities carried out under this section are made readily available on the Office for Gender Equity resource center ORG website; and(B ORG ) widely disseminate the results described in subparagraph (A) to relevant Federal offices, and agencies, educational entities and the general public. SEC ORG . 8 CARDINAL . RESEARCH PERSON AND DEVELOPMENT.(a PERSON ) In General.--The GPE Secretary shall coordinate with the Director of the Institute of Education Sciences ORG and other relevant Federal offices and agencies and entities to investigate, identify, and disseminate best practices to fully implement title IX and reduce and prevent sex SEX discrimination in all areas of education, including--(1) the reduction and prevention of sex SEX stereotyping, bias, and discrimination in curricula, textbooks, software, and other educational materials;(2) the development of policies and programs to--(A) address and prevent sexual and sex SEX -based harassment and violence;(B) ensure that schools and campuses are free from threats to the safety of students, teachers, faculty, and personnel; and(C ORG ) ensure athletic programs are equitable;(3) the development and evaluation of--(A PERSON ) counseling and career guidance training; and(B ORG ) programs to reduce and prevent sex SEX stereotyping, bias, and discrimination;(4) best practices for mitigating implicit bias in teaching, discipline, and all areas of education;(5) best practices for addressing the needs of students who face discrimination based on multiple characteristics, including--(A ORG ) race and color;(B) ethnicity;(C) national origin;(D) disability status;(E) religion;(F) age; or(G) actual or perceived sex SEX ; and(6 ORG ) other activities that the Secretary determines are consistent with the purposes of this Act.(b) Dissemination.--The best practices described under subsection (a) shall be published on the Office for Gender Equity resource center ORG website, as described in section 5(b)(5 LAW ), and the What Works Clearinghouse website of the Institute of Education Sciences ORG . SEC ORG . 9 CARDINAL . REPORT; DISSEMINATION.(a) Report to Congress.--Not later than 2 years DATE after the date of enactment of this Act and every 2 years thereafter, the Secretary shall publish a report on the steps the Department of Education ORG has taken to--(1) support educational entities in fully implementing title IX and reducing and preventing sex SEX discrimination;(2) provide coordination, training, and resources for title IX coordinators and title IX allies to fully carry out their roles and responsibilities; and(3) promote equity in education for students who face discrimination based on multiple characteristics, including--(A ORG ) race and color;(B) ethnicity;(C) national origin;(D) disability status;(E) religion;(F) age; or(G) actual or perceived sex.(b) Dissemination.--The Secretary shall coordinate with the Director of the Institute of Education Sciences ORG and the heads of relevant Federal agencies to ensure that the results of trainings, activities, evaluations, and research developments under this Act are made readily available on the Office for Gender Equity resource center ORG website and disseminated widely to other relevant Federal agencies and offices, educational entities, and the general public. SEC ORG . 10 CARDINAL . RULE OF CONSTRUCTION.Nothing in this Act shall be construed--(1) as modifying any provision of title IX of the Education Amendments ORG of 1972 DATE ( 20 CARDINAL U.S.C. 1681 DATE et seq.); or(2) as affecting the enforcement of such title by the Department of Education ORG , the Department of Justice ORG , or any other Federal agency ORG . SEC ORG . 11 CARDINAL . AUTHORIZATION OF APPROPRIATIONS.(a) In General.--There are authorized to be appropriated to carry out this Act $ 160,000,000 MONEY for each of fiscal years 2024 through 2028.(b DATE ) Use.--From amounts made available to carry out this Act for each fiscal year DATE , the Secretary shall use not less than $140,000,000 MONEY of such amounts to award grants under section 7. &lt;all&gt;</pre></body></html>"b"Congressional Bills 118th CongressFrom LAW the U.S. Government Publishing OfficeH.R. ORG 36 CARDINAL Introduced in House ORG (IH)&lt;DOC&gt;118th CONGRESS1st Session H. R. 36 PERSON To amend title 18 DATE , United States ORG Code, to increase certain penaltiesfor assaulting, resisting, or impeding certain officers or employees.IN THE HOUSE ORG OF REPRESENTATIVESJanuary 9, 2023Mr DATE . Arrington PERSON (for himself, Mr. Estes PERSON , and Mr. Bishop PERSON of North Carolina GPE ) introduced the following bill; which was referred to theCommittee on the Judiciary A BILLTo amend title 18 DATE , United States ORG Code, to increase certain penaltiesfor assaulting, resisting, or impeding certain officers or employees.Be it enacted by the Senate ORG and House of Representatives ORG of the United States of America GPE in Congress ORG assembled, SECTION 1 LAW . SHORT TITLE.This Act may be cited as the ``Patrick Underwood Federal Law Enforcement Protection Act of 2023'' CARDINAL . SEC ORG . 2. CARDINAL ASSAULTING, RESISTING, OR IMPEDING CERTAIN OFFICERS OR EMPLOYEES.Section 111 CARDINAL of title 18, United States ORG Code PERSON , is amended to read as follows:``Sec. 111 CARDINAL . Assaulting, resisting, or impeding certain officers or employees``(a) In General.--Whoever knowingly--``(1 PERSON ) forcibly assaults, resists, opposes, impedes, intimidates, or interferes while performing official duties,``(2) makes physical contact with or has the intent to commit another felony while acting under paragraph ( 1 CARDINAL ), or``(3) uses a deadly or dangerous weapon against (including a weapon intended to cause death or danger but that fails to do so by reason of a defective component) or inflicts bodily injury while acting under paragraph ( 1),any CARDINAL officer or employee of the United States GPE or of any agency in any branch of the United States Government ORG (including any member of the uniformed services) while engaged in or on account of the performance of official duties, or any person who formerly served as a person defined above on account of the performance of official duties during such person's term of service shall be punished as provided in subsection (b).``(b) Punishment.--A violation of subsection (a) shall be punished as follows:``(1) For a violation of paragraph ( 1 CARDINAL ) of such subsection, by a fine under this title of not less than $1,000 MONEY or by imprisonment for not more than 1 year DATE , or both.``(2) For a violation of paragraph ( 2 CARDINAL ) of such subsection, by a fine under this title of not less than $2,500 MONEY or by imprisonment for not more than 10 years DATE , or both.``(3) For a violation of paragraph ( 3 CARDINAL ) of such subsection, by a fine under this title of not less than $10,000 MONEY or by mandatory imprisonment, or both.''. &lt;all&gt;</pre></body></html>"b"Congressional Bills 118th CongressFrom PERSON the U.S. Government Publishing ORG OfficeH.R. 561 Introduced in House ORG (IH)&lt;DOC&gt;118th CONGRESS1st SessionH. R. 561 To ensure affordable abortion coverage and care for every person, and for other purposes.IN THE HOUSE ORG OF REPRESENTATIVESJanuary 26, 2023 Ms. Lee PERSON of California GPE (for herself, Ms. DeGette PERSON , Ms. Schakowsky PERSON , Ms. Pressley PERSON , Mr. Schiff PERSON , Mr. Smith PERSON of Washington GPE , Mr. Espaillat PERSON , Mr. Green PERSON of Texas GPE , Ms. Adams PERSON , Mr. Bera PERSON , Mr. Carson PERSON , Ms. Salinas PERSON , Mr. Kim PERSON of NewJersey ORG , Ms. Craig PERSON , Ms. Kuster PERSON , Ms. Balint PERSON , Ms. McCollum PERSON , Mr. Foster PERSON , Mrs. Watson Coleman PERSON , Mr. Schneider PERSON , Mr. Sherman PERSON , Mr. Higgins PERSON of NewYork ORG , Ms. Pettersen PERSON , Mr. Ruppersberger PERSON , Ms. Pingree PERSON , Mr. Deluzio PERSON , Mr. Pappas PERSON , Mr. Allred PERSON , Ms. Bush PERSON , Mr. Goldman PERSON of New York GPE , Mr. Kildee PERSON , Mr. Davis PERSON of Illinois GPE , Mr. Soto PERSON , Mr. Cicilline PERSON , Mr. Trone PERSON , Mr. Phillips PERSON ,Mrs. Dingell PERSON , Ms. Wasserman Schultz PERSON , Ms. Ross PERSON , Mr. Kilmer PERSON , Ms. Titus PERSON , Mr. Payne PERSON , Mr. Beyer PERSON , Ms. Matsui PERSON , Mr. Evans PERSON , Mr. Blumenauer PERSON , Mr. Case PERSON ,Ms. Norton PERSON , Mr. Cleaver PERSON , Mr. Swalwell PERSON , Mr. Pallone PERSON , Ms. Wilson ofFlorida PERSON , Mr. Connolly PERSON , Mrs. Napolitano PERSON , Ms. Meng PERSON , Mr. Casar PERSON , Mr. Stanton PERSON , Mr. Meeks PERSON , Ms. Moore PERSON of Wisconsin GPE , Ms. Stevens PERSON , Mr. Johnson PERSON ofGeorgia, Ms. Scholten PERSON , Ms. Omar PERSON , Mr. Correa PERSON , Mrs. Hayes PERSON , Mr. Auchincloss PERSON , Mr. Bowman PERSON , Mr. McGovern PERSON , Mr. Huffman PERSON , Mr. Moskowitz PERSON , Ms. Crockett PERSON , Mr. Crow PERSON , Mr. Jackson PERSON of North Carolina GPE , Ms. Wexton PERSON , Mr. Nadler PERSON , Mr. Garcia PERSON of Illinois GPE , Ms. Tokuda PERSON , Mr. Himes PERSON , Mr. Gomez PERSON , Mr. Panetta PERSON , Mr. Castro PERSON of Texas GPE , Mr. Neguse PERSON , Mr. Larson PERSON of Connecticut GPE , Mr. Garamendi PERSON , Mr. Sarbanes PERSON , Mr. Morelle PERSON , Mr. Gottheimer PERSON , Mrs. Beatty PERSON , Mr. Vargas PERSON , Ms. Chu PERSON , Ms. Brownley PERSON , Ms. Castor PERSON of Florida GPE , Ms. Manning PERSON , Ms. Porter PERSON , Mr. Mullin PERSON , Ms. Schrier PERSON , Mr. Mfume PERSON , Ms. Underwood PERSON , Ms. Sanchez PERSON , Ms. Blunt Rochester PERSON , Mrs. Fletcher PERSON , Mr. Doggett PERSON , Ms. Lois Frankel PERSON of Florida GPE , Mrs. Trahan PERSON , Mrs. McBath PERSON , Ms. Dean PERSON of Pennsylvania GPE , Mr. Veasey PERSON , Ms. Kaptur PERSON , Ms. Strickland PERSON , Mr. DeSaulnier PERSON , Mr. Pocan PERSON , Mr. Takano PERSON , Ms. Scanlon PERSON , Mr. Cartwright PERSON , Ms. Stansbury PERSON , Mr. Levin PERSON , Mr. Quigley PERSON , Mr. Thompson PERSON of California GPE , Ms. Sherrill PERSON , Ms. Barragan PERSON , Ms. Williams PERSON of Georgia GPE , Ms. Budzinski PERSON , Mrs. Torres PERSON of California GPE , Ms. Velazquez PERSON , Mr. Ryan PERSON , Mr. Tonko PERSON , Mr. Aguilar PERSON , Ms. Jayapal PERSON , Mr. Krishnamoorthi PERSON , Ms. Tlaib PERSON , Mr. Grijalva PERSON , Mr. Ruiz PERSON , Mr. Larsen PERSON of Washington GPE , Mr. Torres PERSON of New York GPE , Mr. Khanna PERSON , Mr. Menendez PERSON , Mr. Scottof Virginia PERSON , Mr. Robert Garcia PERSON of California GPE , Ms. Kelly PERSON of Illinois GPE , Ms. DeLauro PERSON , Mr. Gallego PERSON , Mr. Carbajal PERSON , Ms. Jacobs PERSON , Mr. Peters PERSON , Mr. Casten PERSON , Mr. Moulton PERSON , Ms. Davids PERSON of Kansas GPE , Mrs. Cherfilus-McCormick PERSON ,Ms. Jackson Lee PERSON , Ms. Brown PERSON , Mr. Cohen PERSON , Ms. Wild PERSON , Mrs. Lee PERSON of Nevada GPE , Ms. DelBene PERSON , Ms. Bonamici PERSON , Ms. Kamlager-Dove PERSON , Ms. Garcia PERSON of Texas GPE , Mr. Lieu PERSON , Ms. Leger Fernandez PERSON , Mr. Cardenas PERSON , Mr. Carter PERSON of Louisiana GPE , Ms. Hoyle PERSON of Oregon GPE , Mrs. Foushee PERSON , Ms. Escobar PERSON , Mr. Keating PERSON , Ms. Clarke PERSON ofNew York, Ms. Ocasio-Cortez PERSON , Ms. Pelosi PERSON , and Ms. Lofgren PERSON ) introducedthe following bill; which was referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce ORG , and in addition to the Committees on Ways and Means, Natural Resources ORG , Armed Services ORG , Veterans' Affairs ORG , the Judiciary, Oversight and Accountability, and Foreign Affairs ORG , for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committeeconcerned A BILLTo ensure affordable abortion coverage and care for every person, and for other purposes.Be it enacted by the Senate ORG and House of Representatives ORG of the United States of America GPE in Congress ORG assembled, SECTION 1 LAW . SHORT TITLE.This Act may be cited as the ``Equal Access to Abortion Coverage in Health Insurance Act of PRODUCT 2023 DATE '' or the ``EACH Act PRODUCT of 2023'' CARDINAL . SEC ORG . 2 CARDINAL . FINDINGS.Congress makes the following findings:(1) All people should have access to abortion services regardless of actual or perceived race, color, ethnicity, language, ancestry, citizenship, immigration status, sex SEX (including a sex SEX stereotype; pregnancy, childbirth, or a related medical condition; sexual orientation SEXUALITY or gender GENDER identity; and sex SEX characteristics), age, disability, or sex SEX work status or behavior.(2) A person's income level, wealth, or type of insurance should not prevent them from having access to a full range of pregnancy-related health care, including abortion services.(3) No person should have the decision to have, or not to have, an abortion made for them based on the ability or inability to afford the health care service.(4) Since 1976 DATE , the Federal Government ORG has banned the use of Federal funds to pay for abortion services and allows for exceptions only in very narrow circumstances. This ban affects people of reproductive age in the United States GPE who are insured through the Medicaid ORG program, as well as individuals who receive insurance or care through other federally funded health programs and plans.(5) Women make up the majority of Medicaid ORG enrollees ( 54 percent PERCENT ) and, in 2019 DATE , approximately 14 million CARDINAL women of reproductive age relied on the program for care. Due to systematic barriers and discrimination, a disproportionately higher number of women of color and Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual ORG , Transgender GPE , or Queer PERSON ( LGBTQ IDENTITY ) individuals are enrolled in the program.(6) Women of color are more likely to be insured by the Medicaid ORG program. Nationwide, 29 percent PERCENT of Black women and 25 percent PERCENT of Hispanic NORP women aged 15-49 DATE were enrolled in Medicaid ORG in 2018 DATE , compared with 15 percent PERCENT of White women.(7) In the aggregate, nearly one-fifth CARDINAL ( 19 percent PERCENT ) of Asian American NORP and Pacific Islander LOC women are enrolled in the Medicaid ORG program, while enrollment rates for certain Asian NORP ethnic subgroups are much higher (at 62 percent PERCENT of Bhutanese NORP women, 43 percent PERCENT of Hmong NORP women and 32 percent PERCENT of Pakistani NORP women).(8) Medicaid ORG also provides coverage to more than one CARDINAL in four CARDINAL ( 27 percent PERCENT ) nonelderly American NORP Indian NORP and Alaska Native PERSON ( AIAN ORG ) adults and half CARDINAL of AIAN ORG children.(9 PERSON ) In a 2014 DATE nationwide survey of LGBT IDENTITY people with incomes less than 400 percent PERCENT Federal Poverty Level ORG ( FPL ORG ), 61 percent PERCENT of all respondents had incomes in the Medicaid ORG expansion range--up to 138 percent PERCENT of the FPL ORG --including 73 percent PERCENT of African-American NORP respondents, 67 percent PERCENT of Latino respondents, and 53 percent PERCENT of White respondents. Another survey found that 32 percent PERCENT of Asian NORP and Native Hawaiian/Pacific Islander ORG transgender people were living in poverty.(10) Of women aged 15-44 DATE enrolled in Medicaid ORG in 2018 DATE , 55 percent PERCENT lived in the 34 States and the District of Columbia GPE where Medicaid ORG does not cover abortion services except in limited circumstances. This amounted to 7.2 million CARDINAL women of reproductive age, including 3 million CARDINAL women living below the FPL ORG . Of this population, Black, Indigenous GPE , and other People of Color ( BIPOC ORG ) women accounted for 51 percent PERCENT of those enrolled.(11) The Indian Health Service ORG ( IHS ORG ) is the federally funded health program for American Indians NORP and Alaska Natives PERSON . The IHS ORG serves a population of approximately 2.56 million CARDINAL and as a federally funded system, since 1988 DATE , it has been barred from providing abortion services except for very limited cases. American Indians NORP and Alaska GPE Natives often face higher levels of poverty and limited access to health care for a number of intersecting oppressions thus leaving them without recourse for the Federal ban on abortion services.(12 ORG ) Moreover, 26 CARDINAL States also prohibit coverage of abortion services in the marketplaces and 11 CARDINAL prohibit coverage in private health insurance plans under the Patient Protection ORG and Affordable Care Act of 2010 DATE (Public Law 111 CARDINAL -148).(13) A recent report details how restrictions on abortion services coverage interfere with a person's individual decisionmaking, with their health and well-being, with their economic security, with their vulnerability to intimate partner violence, and with their constitutionally protected right to a safe and normal health care service.(14) About 25 percent PERCENT of women covered by Medicaid ORG seeking abortion services must carry their pregnancies to term because they are unable to obtain funds for their care. Government-imposed barriers to abortion services restrict people's decisions on if, when, and how to parent, and have long-lasting and life-altering harmful effects on the pregnant person, their families and their communities. Those who seek and are denied abortion services are more likely to remain in or fall into poverty than those who access the care they need.(15) Restrictions on abortion service coverage have a disproportionately harmful impact on women with low incomes, women of color, immigrant women, LGBTQ IDENTITY people, and young women. Additionally, numerous state-imposed barriers make it disparately difficult for low-income people, people of color, immigrants, LGBTQ IDENTITY people, and young people to access the health care and resources necessary to prevent unintended pregnancy or to assure that they are able to carry healthy pregnancies to term. Furthermore, young people of reproductive age ( 15-24 DATE ) are more likely to have a lower income than those older than that, and this income gap is greater for young BIPOC ORG . More than 40 percent PERCENT of youth and children under age 19 DATE and almost a quarter of young people age 19 to 25 DATE have health insurance through government programs. Without insurance coverage for abortion services, young people are at greater risk of not having the economic means to afford care outside of insurance. Young people face disproportionate access barriers to abortion including parental involvement requirement (notification and consent) and cost, in addition to barriers to contraception and inadequate and incomplete sexual(ity) education. These challenges, which are magnified for BIPOC ORG and queer IDENTITY , trans GENDER , and nonbinary GENDER youth, can cause significant delays in access to needed care, and could ultimately harm the life of the young person seeking abortion services.
